summary: DHH recounts how Apple threatened to remove HEY, Basecamp's new email service, from the App Store on its launch day unless they paid Apple's 30% revenue cut through in-app purchases. Despite HEY following the same model as Gmail, Netflix, and other apps that sell subscriptions outside the App Store, Apple singled them out. After a viral public campaign, Apple partially relented but forced HEY to create an unnecessary free tier. DHH argues Apple wields monopoly power over the digital economy since 90% of HEY's paying customers use Apple devices, making App Store access existential. He calls on Congress to force Apple to allow alternative payment processing and restore competition.
key_points: Apple threatened to remove HEY from the App Store on launch day despite approving it days earlier, demanding 30% of subscription revenue through in-app purchases
Apple enforces its rules selectively — Gmail, Netflix, and banks follow the same subscription model HEY originally used but face no consequences
90% of HEY's paying customers use Apple devices, making App Store access an existential business requirement rather than an optional distribution channel
The disparity is stark: Facebook pays Apple $99/year while HEY would owe $300,000/year on $1M in revenue, punishing paid services while rewarding surveillance-based free ones
DHH's proposed fix is forcing Apple to allow alternative payment processors and the freedom to tell customers about them
money_quotes: Apple controls who can and cannot compete in virtually all sectors of the digital economy through the monopoly power they wield capriciously with the App Store.
Apple isn't just a dominant player in this market, they are the market. Without access to the iPhone, we're dead.

We were sitting on a rocket ship of a successful new service, eager to hire more people, but were at the same time essentially paralyzed from making any investments, because what if Apple wouldn't give us the permission to exist?

A company like Facebook, that makes tens of billions of dollars every year from their iOS app, simply has to pay Apple the $99/year developer license. But our HEY service would have to pay Apple $300,000/year, if we made but a million dollars?
What other small software company is going to invest years of effort and millions of dollars in a new service, if Apple might kill them dead on their birthday.
This is how you stifle entrepreneurship, depress innovation, and prevent the next generation of makers from ever having a chance to compete.
Imagine if the telcos demanded a cut of company revenues, since they provide the phone line that connects customers, in the heyday of their monopoly?
key_insight: Apple's monopoly control over app distribution lets it selectively enforce rules to extract rent from small competitors while exempting big tech incumbents, effectively functioning as a gatekeeper that stifles entrepreneurship and innovation.
